narrative: As a Black male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and personal circumstances to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 29 (well under the 36.7 average), and low decile rankings in Year 1 and Year 3 grades at school—and calculated an 88.3% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their standards. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers and strongly signaled potential struggles ahead compared to typical students who rank around the middle. Next in importance was your Year 1 school decile of 1, again at the very bottom compared to the average fifth-place standing, reinforcing concerns about early academic performance. While your undergraduate GPA provided some counterbalance by slightly favoring a pass prediction—since 2.90, though below average, wasn't as detrimental as your school deciles—it wasn't enough to offset the top two factors. Your full-time student status, common among 93% of applicants, and middle family income didn't sway the outcome much either. Overall, your profile stood out as riskier than most, with those low school deciles driving the decision more than your LSAT or other details. This doesn't define your potential, especially given the unique challenges many Black men face in competitive academic pipelines. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, strengthening your recent grades to climb those school deciles, or exploring targeted prep programs for the bar that address early weaknesses. Building a stronger academic narrative through additional coursework or community college excellence could also help in reapplying to schools with more flexible criteria. With focused effort, you can shift these predictions in your favor next time.
